item.content:encoded: Fuck yeah haha yes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's also a coincidence that the original 4Chan post of SCP-173 was created sometime in July 2007?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is "ubique" a typo, or a clever thing I'm not getting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sees the large number of upvotes.

Sees “click to enlarge” on the caption.

Overcome by a large sense of dread.

Sees filename.

Dread intensifies.

***

And then I actually read the article. I think I’ve been reading too many entries that use the picture to mess with you lately.

Very nicely done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably one of my favourites.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: It makes more sense to link SCP-2000 back here. In fact I am strongly in favor of that link.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I like this better. Will edit accordingly.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
I think this could do without the 2000 link. In the interest of linking to something similar a reader might enjoy (if indeed they enjoy this article) I recommend the following:

alternatively, numerous anomalies similar to SCP-1422 may exist,

or perhaps:

alternatively, numerous anomalies similar to SCP-1422 may exist

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: I still strongly oppose this crosslink.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: No opinion

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: While this particular crosslink is not truly within the intent of the crosslink initiative, it is within my opinion that that particular crosslink is a relatively logical conclusion. It's frankly not the best crosslink there is, but not all crosslinks are equal, I guess.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
For me, how subtle the reference might be is beside the point. The cross-link project is designed to allow readers to move smoothly from more popular articles (that they are likely to read early on) towards less well-known articles, to encourage them to keep reading through the wiki.

I don't think that linking to SCP-2000, a contest winner with over 800 votes, and one of the most well-known articles on the site, achieves this aim.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: One of the Resurrection tales involves this too, IIRC.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the critisism. I have made it less obvious, while still keeping the mystery of 1422, thank you.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Before it was subtle, now it's too obvious. I do not support this, I like creative crosslinks.

Plus, this would imply 2000 caused this. It was better left ambiguous.

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer stopped. Edits made.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ show block
– hide block

Changing this:

alternatively, numerous anomalies similar to SCp-1422 may exist,

To this:

alternatively, numerous anomalies similar to SCP-1422 may exist,
Congratulations, 1422. You deserved it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sarcasm?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But what about all of the memetic SCPs on this site that would otherwise infect us? What if I'm just a Foundation disinformation agent?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What kind of idiot doesn't know about the Yellowstone supervolcano?

(Not a criticism, just an amusing coincidence.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean, SCP-2000?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idea: It's THE FOUNDATION keeping Yellowstone from existing, because of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagine there are few if any references to most of the National Parks and Wildernesses, even the famous ones. Is there an article that includes Yosemite or Sequoia/King's Canyon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it interesting that this site somehow successfully avoided mentioning the Yellowstone National Park in all articles until the creation of this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might be written well before SCP-2000 came along, but it's sure convenient. I guess we now know when the current iteration of the Foundation found SCP-2000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that's fucking weird

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: HOLY. SHIT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Junior Researcher Scranton…Scranton Reality Anchors on SCP-2000…hmmm…weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OR SCP-2000 and SCP-1422 were created in parallel to hide the existence of SCP-2000 from foundation personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if it's just a crazy coincidence?
the foundation already knows about the existence of parallel universes right? so what if this foundation just exists in a universe where the ridiculously small chance of nobody in the foundation knowing about Yellowstone park is reality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2000
That heavily references this. In my headcanon, the Foundation had to do severe renovations or was in the process of building it when this happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if the Foundation tried to erase knowledge of a memetic SCP from all staff members, but the process was sloppy and also removed knowledge of Yellowstone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit, this SCP is scary. Just think, what if who/whatever created this effect has been using it to suppress our knowledge of other things? We'd never know.
Hold on a minute.
What if the reason why we believe the Foundation isn't real is because the Foundation is utilizing this SCP? What if this site actually is made up of leaked documents, and there are breaches all the goddamn time, but the Foundation has used this SCP to remove our belief in them and any SCPs that ever escaped, despite the huge number of deaths they cause? It's pretty damn far-fetched… but hey. Maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1422 was created by a conscious agency in order to conceal activity in or near Yellowstone National Park from the Foundation. Investigation into various groups and persons of interest has not shown conclusively that any individuals or organizations are capable of creating or using an anomaly such as SCP-1422; however, as this hypothetical individual or group could be expected to possess the means to prevent the Foundation from discovering them, this is not unexpected.

The group is SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the tallest building in the world?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some anomaly made you both forget that you're the same person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagine trying to test this to find other things we don't know but should.

"Alright sir, we're just gonna find out if you know anything that we should know."

"I haven't done any—" "No, no. We mean anything that anyone should know, and we just don't. [ELABORATE EXPLANATION]."

"Oh, ok. Do you know, um, chicken? Like, food?" "Yes."

"Do you know… democracy?" "We are familiar, yes."

"Do you know Elton John?" "… Who?"

Addendum 1422-4: As of 12/28/2013, Elton John was successfully terminated by MTF Tau-7, Rocket Men.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How did I miss this? (Ironyyy)

There's nothing scarier than finding out you don't know something you don't know. +1

(Also this is better than Samothrace.)

(Also I never liked Samothrace.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously one of my favorites. So dastardly in its simplicity. No need to go for shock value or "break the fourth wall" in any mind-bending, meta way. "It" was there, and now "it" doesn't seem to be. And yet, so many questions remain. +1 indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't tell if you're serious or this is a joke…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(and that's the only thing I dislike. Make up a name that doesn't break my immersion!)

NEVER

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hnngh, I loved this, and my personal explanation for this is it's a test run. Why did Scranton (and that's the only thing I dislike. Make up a name that doesn't break my immersion!) discover Yellowstone when he did? The test run had been found to be successful, and was no longer necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is Yellowstone? This is the first time I've heard of it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. Loved this SCP, for all the aforementioned reasons.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Apparently it's this thing out in Wyoming? Bunch of geysers and shit.
Congratulations on this, though I haven't the slightest clue what sort of park your article is referencing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that it would be bad science to jump to conclusions about what many objects appear to be, but is it reasonable to assume that these parallels would be completely ignored? The possible biblical/historical implications of Cain, Able, and Lilith are never acknowledged in their respective articles. There are also cases like SCP-746 where the mythological parallel could reasonably have been used as a much simpler visual cue for in-universe readers ("is similar in appearance to traditional depictions of Tengu in Japanese folklore"), but were not mentioned at all.

At minimum the old myths would make a reasonable frame of reference for testing: no, we should not assume that the wolfman has a silver allergy based only on folklore, but we sure as hell SHOULD be investigating the possibility. The fact that we're not doing so for the most part strikes me as very strange, but is also one of the most interesting quirks in the Foundation, especially when an article like this pops up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this. My own personal headcanon wants to believe that the reason the Foundation couldn't detect Yellowstone is because whatever was there didn't want to be detected. And now that it's gone, Yellowstone can once again be remembered.

But what was there? And where are they now? Perhaps we know, and just can't remember…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me wonder: is Junior Researcher Scranton still stuck inside SCP-████, which has somehow copied his own universe's Foundation and placed it in a universe in which Yellowstone exists?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a shame I can't upvote twice. This is my favorite kind of SCP: unexplained and paranoia inducing. Very, very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, yes I am.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It arbitrarily needed to be some time after computers became commonplace, but I like to set my SCPs at least a little in the past.

Crazy coincidence, eh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scantron, did you pick July 9, 2007 arbitrarily, or is that when you visited? I have spent exactly one day of my life in Yellowstone. That one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first transit stop to New Washington on Kepler-22b. An associate of mine has a building company there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very subtle and inventive.

I especially love the uncertainty it presents. Since there is no surefire way to determine if this is still active, ramifications are powerful. Is Foundation infiltrated? Does it run in a controlled environment? There could be literally an entire galactic empire out there - and all requires that the Foundation doesn't know about their embassy and facilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. Anomalous items and SCP objects are alien and uncomprehensible (at least their method of functioning). Applying frameworks forged from our cultural legacy (myths) to them without scientific method will fail. Foundation uses rituals and "magic" only when they are proven to work (like the Montauk procedure).

The "divine fallen angel" is scientifically unfalsifiable, making the myth interpretation only one of the possibilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well… the response is small not because they aren't afraid1, it's because there's not really a lot they CAN do.

The tone of the piece is deliberately kept as neutral as possible to make sure that any horror that comes through stays as fridge horror — just like it would have for the Foundation personnel.

Footnotes
1. They definitely are — the hypotheses that Graff mentions in Addendum 3 were brought forward by multiple personnel, they made the task force high-priority, and heavily classified all the info on the phenomenon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I very much enjoyed this but have to say that I believe the foundation would have a far more serious reaction than that little addendum at the end. Finding out that people may have the capability of wiping entire things from the minds of every member of foundation staff and it just gets a little "ahh well, we'll just presume it's neutralised" seems a little unrealistic for me.
